artifacts.sourceTitle: UW-Madison Guiding Principles for Generative AI in Teaching
Generative AI Services at UW-Madison: Tools, Policies & Resources
Statement on use of generative AI

artifacts.claimText: UW-Madison publishes an official generative AI use and policies page that says faculty, staff, students, and affiliates must follow UW-Madison, UW System Administration, and Board of Regents policies when using generative AI tools and services.
artifacts.normalizedValue: University-wide AI use policy guidance exists

citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: All university faculty, staff, students and affiliates must follow these policies. As with everything you do at the university, you must follow UW-Madison, UW System Administration (UWSA) and UW System Board of Regents policies when using generative AI tools and services.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: The page states that all university community groups listed must follow the AI-use policies.
artifacts.rightsNote: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.notes: The claim is narrowly scoped to the existence and stated audience of the official UW-Madison AI use and policies page.

artifacts.claimText: UW-Madison states that sensitive, restricted, or otherwise protected data may not be entered into a generative AI tool or service unless the tool has undergone appropriate internal review.
artifacts.normalizedValue: Protected data requires reviewed AI tool before entry

citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: You may not enter any sensitive, restricted or otherwise protected data into any generative AI tool or service unless it has undergone appropriate internal review.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: Protected institutional data is barred from generative AI tools unless the tool has received internal review.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Section: Prohibited use and relevant policies
artifacts.rightsNote: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.notes: The claim follows the page's unless-reviewed condition and does not broaden the data categories beyond the source wording.

artifacts.claimText: UW-Madison identifies use of AI-generated code in institutional IT systems or services without human review for malicious elements as a prohibited use.
artifacts.normalizedValue: AI-generated institutional code requires human review for malicious elements

artifacts.claimText: UW-Madison's CTLM generative AI teaching guidance says instructors and administrators are responsible for protecting student privacy and intellectual work, securing FERPA-protected data, and following university policies that apply to generative AI.
artifacts.normalizedValue: Teaching guidance emphasizes privacy, intellectual work, FERPA, and applicable policies

artifacts.sourceTitle: UW-Madison Guiding Principles for Generative AI in Teaching
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Instructors and administrators are responsible for protecting student privacy and intellectual work, as well as securing FERPA-protected data. They must also follow university policies that apply to the use of generative AI.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: The teaching guidance assigns privacy, intellectual-work, and FERPA-protected data responsibilities to instructors and administrators.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Section: Protect Data and Intellectual Property

artifacts.claimText: UW-Madison Student Conduct guidance says students are responsible for knowing their instructor's expectations for AI tools and should ask before using AI if permission is unclear.
artifacts.normalizedValue: Students must know or ask about instructor AI expectations

citation.publisher: UW-Madison Office of Student Conduct and Community Standards
citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Students are responsible for knowing their instructor's expectations when it comes to using AI tools. If it is unclear whether AI tools are allowed in a particular course or for an assignment, it is the student's responsibility to ask their instructor before using them.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: Student Conduct places responsibility on students to know instructor expectations for AI tool use.

artifacts.claimText: UW-Madison Registrar guidance says UW-Madison-provided AI meeting summarization tools are generally approved for use with FERPA data, but unit leadership decides whether and how they may be used.
artifacts.normalizedValue: FERPA use of AI meeting summarization tools is generally approved subject to unit leadership

citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: UW-Madison-provided AI meeting summarization tools are generally approved for use with FERPA data. Unit leadership decides whether the tools may be used, including restrictions on the types of meetings it can be used for.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: Registrar guidance generally approves UW-Madison-provided AI meeting summarization tools for FERPA data.

artifacts.claimText: UW-Madison CTLM encourages instructors to communicate expectations for students' use of generative AI tools early and throughout a course, including through syllabi, Canvas, and course discussion.
artifacts.normalizedValue: Instructors encouraged to communicate AI expectations

citation.publisher: UW-Madison Center for Teaching, Learning and Mentoring
citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: UW-Madison encourages instructors to share their expectations regarding students' use of generative artificial intelligence (AI) tools both at the beginning of a course and throughout the semester. Instructors may communicate their expectations to students in a variety of ways such as via their syllabi, in Canvas, and during course discussions and activities.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: CTLM encourages instructors to share expectations about students' use of generative AI tools.

artifacts.title: University of Wisconsin-Madison Generative AI Policy Report - 2026-05-14
artifacts.summary: UW-Madison has official public guidance for generative AI use and policies, teaching guidance, student academic integrity expectations, FERPA-related AI guidance, and university-provided AI tool categories. The staged claims are narrow candidates tied to official UW-Madison pages and are not canonical public conclusions.
limitations: This report is based on public official sources retrieved on 2026-05-14.
Login-gated NetID guidance was not accessed and was documented as rejected/inaccessible.
Program-level or secondary library guide pages were documented but not used as university-wide evidence.
This tracker is not legal advice, not academic integrity advice, and not an official university statement unless a linked source is the university's own official page.
artifacts.sourceRightsPolicy: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.
